share.share_text: The video starts off looking normal enough: a man spraying what seems like cockroach spray all over a floor crawling with roaches. Then the twist hitsâthose âcockroachesâ are actually tiny firecrackers. He casually lights one, quickly drops a box over the whole mess, and suddenly the box starts jumping and rattling as loud pops explode underneath. The sudden chaos turns the scene from gross to shocking to hilarious in seconds, making it one of those unexpected, blink-and-you-miss-it comedy moments.

Shark Tank is a reality TV series where entrepreneurs pitch business ideas to a panel of wealthy investors, known as âsharks,â in hopes of securing funding in exchange for equity. The show is based on the Japanese format Dragonâs Den and first aired in the United States in 2009, going on to win multiple Emmy Awards for its impact and popularity.

The series has helped launch or scale major consumer brands such as Scrub Daddy and Bombas, while also shaping public interest in startups, venture capital, and entrepreneurship. Its format has been replicated globally, reflecting how investor-driven pitching has become a mainstream part of modern business culture.

The traditional entry-level landscape has shifted into a "low-hire, low-fire" environment, where the bottom rungs of the career ladder are increasingly automated. Recent 2026 data from LinkedIn and Indeed shows that while overall hiring remains sluggish due to macro factors, the share of job postings labeled "entry-level" has dropped to record lows. Companies are pivoting toward "agentic workflows," using AI to handle the data entry, basic coding, and research tasks that once served as the primary training ground for new graduates.

This structural change has created a "paper ceiling" where even junior roles now frequently demand years of experience or specialized AI mastery. Employers increasingly prioritize "AI-augmented" candidates who can manage multi-agent systems and deliver mid-level productivity from their first day on the job. While high-level strategic roles are growing, the vanishing of routine starter tasks means that newcomers must now possess the judgment of a veteran just to get through the door.

Is the career ladder missing its most important step?

Thanos isnât just a villain, heâs a philosophy wrapped in armor. Born on Titan, cursed with intelligence and burdened by foresight, he saw what others ignored: unchecked growth leads to collapse. While his methods were brutal, his conviction was unshakable. To Thanos, genocide wasnât cruelty; it was balance.

What makes him terrifying isnât his strength, but his certainty. He doesnât crave chaos like Loki or power like Ultron. He genuinely believes heâs the hero of the story. Every sacrifice, including his own happiness, is justified in pursuit of a âgrateful universe.â Thatâs why he can sit calmly after wiping out half of existence â mission accomplished.

The Infinity Gauntlet didnât corrupt Thanos; it amplified him. Each stone represented control over reality itself, yet he wielded them with restraint, not rage. Even when he wins, he doesnât celebrate. Thereâs no joy, only duty.

In the end, Thanos forces a hard question: Is peace worth any cost? And thatâs why he remains one of the most compelling antagonists ever, not because he was evil, but because in his mind, he was right.

That feeling of âI canât unsee itâ is actually a real brain phenomenon. Once your brain processes a highly unusual, shocking, or disturbing image or idea, it stores it in your visual memory. Unlike words you can forget, visual memories stick because your brain treats them as potentially important survival infoâyour mind thinks, âHey, remember this, it might matter later!â

The funny (and frustrating) part? You canât just âeraseâ it. Trying to ignore it often makes it more persistentâthe more you think, the stronger the memory becomes. Thatâs why memes, weird clips, or shocking TikTok scenes live rent-free in your head.

Best coping strategy: replace it with another strong, funny, or neutral image. Your brain will eventually overwrite itâ¦ slowly.

#CantUnsee #BrainMemory #VisualShock #MemeMoments #RentFreeInYourHead

This viral video captures a man repeatedly attempting to flee a pursuing Belgian Malinois through a kennel gate, creating a comedic "infinity loop" as the dog's relentless energy traps him in a cycle of evasion, filmed at what appears to be a dog training facility.
, an account specialising in absurd, decontextualised human moments, the clip amassed over 8 million views and 34,000 likes within weeks, highlighting the breed's renowned staminaâBelgian Malinois can sustain high-intensity activity for up to 30 minutes without fatigue, per veterinary studies on working dogs.
Replies blend humour with practical advice, such as closing the gate firmly or tiring the dog out, underscoring the video's appeal as light-hearted escapism amid everyday frustrations, while sparking discussions on canine behaviour and owner mishaps.

Sam Altman explains a key lesson for founders: donât fear sharing your ideas. Even if your concept is brilliant, most peopleâeven big companiesâare too busy to steal it. You could leave instructions on Tim Cookâs desk and still be safe.

He points out that extreme secrecy among founders is usually a bad sign. You need to talk about your vision to attract investors, talent, and customers. Sharing the broad outline rarely leads to riskâit leads to momentum.

As proof, Y Combinator has 2,500+ competitors, yet holds 180% of the global profits among accelerators. They openly share their playbook, and still no one replicates it effectively. The takeaway: execution beats secrecy.
